With Nd2 White transposes to the King's Indian Attack. The next moves for white are usually Ngf3, g3, Bg2, O-O and Re1, preparing to fight for the center but also looking at the long term plan of directly attacking the king.
Black continues with the mainline French moves whereas white is preparing to enter a King's Indian Attack setup after Nd2. White can also transpose to other openings.
f5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the French Defense.

This is a way for White to avoid the "classical" French Defence. Though White's game looks less active (White will have to fianchetto his king's bishop, leaving time for Black to develop), a powerful attack is possible by advancing the king's pawn to e5, building a strong outpost.

After Black's natural response (exd5) there is a symmetric position on the board, and whatever kind of
an advantage White hopes to get from their next moves Black can usually attain that very same kind
of an advantage in response, by simply copying White's moves. Although this variation has a reputation of being dull and drawish, both White and Black players have
found ways to unbalance the game.

The McCutcheon variation first became well-known as the result of an 1885 simultaneous exhibition game played in New York by World Champion Steinitz as White against John Lindsay McCutcheon, a lawyer from Pennsylvania. McCutcheon won convincingly in just 28 moves, and thus brought attention to the variation.

In the French defence, Black's pawn chain makes it hard to develop the queen's bishop actively. Bd7, Black intends to resolve this liability by trading off their "bad" bishop for White's good one.

Castles queenside, placing the king on the flank while activating the rook toward the center in the French Defense.
Nc6 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the French Defense.
Qd2 activates the queen, increasing pressure in the French Defense.
c5 challenges the center from the flank in the French Defense.
Nf3 develops a piece to an active square in the French Defense.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the French Defense.
f4 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the French Defense.
This is an important branching point in the classical French defence. While the main line /7.
Recapturing with the king would be unnecessarily dangerous, so /6. Qxe7/ is the only real response.
Retreating the knight, preparing to trade bishops. White can choose to initiate the trade with /6.

White attacks the f6 knight and prevents the e-pawn from being taken. Black's knight has three useful retreating moves:

/5.
Because Nf6 is no longer pinned, Black threatens to win the e4-pawn. Any attempt to defend the pawn, e.
Although the pin looks like White is threatening to win the knight by playing 5. e5, Black can defend against this threat by counterattacking the bishop with 5.
Both players engage a fight for the center. Tension will increase even more after /4.
Nc3 is White's most popular move against the French at the top level, and is considered the most challenging option. White develops a piece and defends the e4 pawn.
d5 challenges White's centre. Black would love to take dxe4: how will White answer this threat.
White takes the opportunity to put a second pawn in the centre, and open their bishops and queen for development. As Black prepared with 1.
e6 is the French defence. Black plans to gain space in the centre with their d pawn.
